content:
**Executive summary:** Plants are autotrophs — leaves synthesise food (glucose) by photosynthesis, but roots absorb water and mineral salts from soil.  These substances must reach every cell; waste and food must move to storage and growing regions.

**Must know**
• Diffusion = passive, down concentration gradient, no ATP.
• Leaves make food; roots absorb water — transport links the two.
• Vascular tissue solves the 'tall plant problem' that diffusion cannot.
**Diffusion**
• movement of molecules from a region of **higher concentration** to **lower concentration** down a concentration gradient, until equilibrium. No energy is required; it is a **passive** process.
• Works well in **unicellular algae** and very small/thin plants where every cell is near the environment.
• Too **slow** for tall trees — distance is large, diffusion alone cannot supply water to crown leaves fast enough.
• Higher plants evolved **vascular tissues** (xylem + phloem) for rapid, long-distance transport.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Diffusion vs Osmosis vs Active transport**
| Process | What moves | Energy | Direction |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Diffusion | Any molecules | None (passive) | Down concentration gradient |
| Osmosis | Water only | None (passive) | Through semi-permeable membrane |
| Active transport | Mineral ions | ATP required | Against concentration gradient |


**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Why Plants Need Transport & Diffusion"?
• Explain: Diffusion?
• Explain: Leaves make food; roots absorb water?
• State: Vascular tissue solves the 'tall plant problem' that diffusion cannot.?
• True or false trap: Diffusion ≠ osmosis — osmosis is diffusion of water only through a partially permeabl?
• True or false trap: 'Diffusion needs energy' is FALSE — active transport needs energy, not diffusion.?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Diffusion — spontaneous net movement of particles from high to low concentration.
• Concentration gradient — difference in solute/solvent concentration between two regions.
• Diffusion ≠ osmosis — osmosis is diffusion of **water only** through a partially permeable membrane.
• 'Diffusion needs energy' is FALSE — active transport needs energy, not diffusion.
• Examiners ask: 'Why can't a 30 m tree rely on diffusion?' → distance too great, rate too slow.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• AMOEBA (unicellular): food and gases are exchanged directly through the cell membrane; materials move inside by streaming of cytoplasm.
• OSMOSIS: The movement of water molecules from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.
• Soil water (capillary water) has a higher water concentration than the concentrated cell sap inside the root hair.
• OSMOSIS: Movement of water through a semipermeable membrane from higher to lower water concentration.
• DIFFUSION: Movement of molecules occurs from their higher concentration to their lower concentration (down the gradient); no energy is needed.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-02 — next topic
teacherTip: D-I-F-F-U-S-E: Down the gradient, Inactive (no energy), For fluids and gases, Fast only over short distances.
examTip: Always pair 'diffusion is slow' with 'hence vascular tissues in higher plants' — one-mark linkage questions repeat every year.

content:
**Executive summary:** Osmosis: diffusion of water molecules through a partially (selectively) permeable membrane from a region of higher water concentration (dilute solution / hypotonic side).

**Must know**
• Osmosis = water only + partially permeable membrane.
• Root hairs = one cell thick, no cuticle, maximise absorption area.
• Water moves: soil → root hair → cortex → xylem (still largely osmotic at root level).
**Osmosis**
• diffusion of **water molecules** through a **partially (selectively) permeable membrane** from a region of **higher water concentration** (dilute solution / hypotonic side) to **lower water concentration** (concentrate solution / hypertonic side).

**Root hairs**
• microscopic tubular extensions of **epidermal cells** at root tips; vastly increase **surface area** for absorption.

**Water entry into root hair:**
• Soil water is **dilute** (high water potential); cell sap inside root hair is **more concentrated**.
• Cell membrane acts as partially permeable membrane → water enters root hair by **osmosis**.
• Root hair cell sap becomes dilute → water moves osmotically into adjacent **cortical cells**, then into **xylem vessels**.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Osmosis & Root Hair Cells"?
• Explain: Osmosis?
• Explain: Root hairs?
• State: Water moves: soil → root hair → cortex → xylem (still largely osmotic at ro?
• True or false trap: Water moves into root hair because soil is hypotonic to cell sap — NOT because soil '?
• True or false trap: 'Root hairs are separate organs' is FALSE — they are extensions of epidermal cells.?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Partially permeable membrane — allows water but not all dissolved solutes to pass.
• Root hair — unicellular extension of epidermis, living, no suberin/cuticle.
• Water moves into root hair because soil is **hypotonic** to cell sap — NOT because soil 'pushes' water.
• 'Root hairs are separate organs' is FALSE — they are extensions of epidermal cells.
• Plasmolysis (shrinking of protoplast) occurs if cell is placed in hypertonic solution — reverse of normal root uptake.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• OSMOSIS: The movement of water molecules from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.
• Soil water (capillary water) has a higher water concentration than the concentrated cell sap inside the root hair.
• OSMOSIS: Movement of water through a semipermeable membrane from higher to lower water concentration.
• Soil water enters root hair cells by OSMOSIS (semipermeable membrane).
• Root hairs are long and thin with a large surface area, have a semi-permeable membrane, and their cell sap is of higher concentration than the surrounding soil water — all three features promote water absorption.
• Root hairs are suited for water absorption due to their large surface area (P) and semi-permeable cell membrane (R).

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-01 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch1-03 — next topic
teacherTip: O-S-M-O-S-I-S: Only water, Semi-permeable membrane, Moves to where solutes are more concentrated (water leaves dilute side).
examTip: Diagram labels: root hair, cortex, endodermis, xylem — examiners deduct marks for calling root hair a 'root' instead of epidermal extension.

content:
**Executive summary:** Mineral ions (K⁺, NO₃⁻, PO₄³⁻, Mg²⁺, etc. ) are often more concentrated inside root cells than in soil water.

**Must know**
• Active transport = against gradient + needs energy.
• Minerals dissolved in soil water; insoluble minerals are useless until weathered/dissolved.
• Uptake is selective — plant takes only needed ions.
**Active transport**
• uptake of ions **against** the concentration gradient, using **energy (ATP)** from cellular respiration, through **carrier proteins** in the cell membrane.

**Pathway after uptake:**
• Ions absorbed by root epidermis/root hairs via active transport.
• Passed cell-to-cell through cortex → enter **xylem vessels** with water.
• Transported upward in xylem stream to leaves and other organs.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Active Transport & Mineral Uptake"?
• Explain: Active transport?
• State: Minerals dissolved in soil water; insoluble minerals are useless until weat?
• Explain: Uptake is selective?
• True or false trap: 'Minerals diffuse into roots from soil' is usually FALSE when soil concentration is l?
• Exam focus: Assertion–reason favourite: 'Minerals are absorbed by active transport BECAUSE their conce?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Active transport — energy-dependent movement of substances against a concentration gradient.
• Mineral salt — inorganic ion required for plant nutrition (not organic food).
• Water enters by osmosis (passive); minerals often by active transport (active).
• 'Minerals diffuse into roots from soil' is usually FALSE when soil concentration is lower.
• Metabolic inhibitors (e.g. lack of O₂) reduce active transport but not osmosis significantly.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• Soil water (capillary water) has a higher water concentration than the concentrated cell sap inside the root hair.
• AMOEBA (unicellular): food and gases are exchanged directly through the cell membrane; materials move inside by streaming of cytoplasm.
• Minerals are more concentrated inside the root than in the soil, so they are absorbed against the gradient by active transport, which needs energy (ATP).
• OSMOSIS: The movement of water molecules from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.
• OSMOSIS: Movement of water through a semipermeable membrane from higher to lower water concentration.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-02 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch1-04 — next topic
teacherTip: A-T-P powers Active Transport — if respiration stops, mineral uptake slows even when water still enters.
examTip: Assertion–reason favourite: 'Minerals are absorbed by active transport BECAUSE their concentration is higher inside the root cell' — both parts true.

content:
**Executive summary:** Xylem: complex vascular tissue for upward (unidirectional) transport of water + mineral salts from roots → stem → leaves.

**Must know**
• Xylem = water + minerals only; direction = upward (root to leaf).
• Tracheids + vessels = main water channels.
• Lignin gives mechanical strength; dead cells = uninterrupted water column.
**Xylem**
• complex **vascular tissue** for **upward** (unidirectional) transport of **water + mineral salts** from roots → stem → leaves.

**Conducting elements (dead at maturity):**
• **Tracheids** — elongated, tapering, lignified walls, pits for lateral movement; found in all vascular plants.
• **Vessels** — shorter, wider, cylindrical tubes placed end-to-end; form continuous channels; more efficient; prominent in **angiosperms**.

**Other xylem cells:** parenchyma (storage), fibres (support).

**Properties:**
• Lignified, **dead** cells — no cytoplasm, no blockage by membranes.
• Form hollow, strong, waterproof pipelines resistant to collapse under tension.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Xylem vs Phloem**
| Feature | Xylem | Phloem |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Transport | Water + mineral salts | Organic food (sucrose) |
| Direction | Upward (root → leaf) | Bidirectional (source → sink) |
| Main cells | Tracheids + vessels (dead) | Sieve tubes + companion cells (living) |
| Experiment proof | Eosin/safranin red stain | Girdling — swelling above ring |


**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Xylem — Structure & Upward Transport"?
• Explain: Xylem?
• Explain: Tracheids + vessels?
• Explain: Lignin gives mechanical strength; dead cells?
• True or false trap: 'Xylem transports food' is FALSE.?
• Exam focus: Label tracheid (tapering ends, pits) vs vessel (wide, open ends) in diagrams — NEET Founda?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Xylem — water-conducting vascular tissue.
• Lignin — waterproof strengthening material in cell walls.
• Xylem vs phloem: xylem = water/minerals/upward/dead; phloem = organic food/bidirectional/living sieve tubes.
• 'Xylem transports food' is FALSE.
• Gymnosperms mainly have tracheids; angiosperms have vessels + tracheids.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• XYLEM: • Transports water and minerals • Direction: upward only (roots → leaves) • Made of dead, hollow cells • Driven by transpiration pull PHLOEM: • Transport
• AMOEBA (unicellular): food and gases are exchanged directly through the cell membrane; materials move inside by streaming of cytoplasm.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-03 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch1-05 — next topic
teacherTip: Xylem eXports water upward — X reminds you it carries waXer (mnemonic stretch) and only upward.
examTip: Label tracheid (tapering ends, pits) vs vessel (wide, open ends) in diagrams — NEET Foundation often shows cross-section MCQs.

content:
**Executive summary:** Phloem: complex vascular tissue transporting dissolved organic food (mainly sucrose + amino acids) from leaves (source) to all parts — roots, fruits, seeds, growing tip.

**Must know**
• Phloem = organic food (not water/minerals).
• Sieve tubes are living but lack nucleus at maturity; companion cells help metabolism.
• Direction is from source (leaf) to sink (root/fruit/apical bud).
**Phloem**
• complex vascular tissue transporting **dissolved organic food** (mainly **sucrose** + amino acids) from **leaves** (source) to **all parts** — roots, fruits, seeds, growing tips (sinks).

**Main elements:**
• **Sieve tubes** — column of **living** cells (sieve tube elements); end walls have **sieve plates** (perforations); cytoplasm present but nucleus absent in mature element.
• **Companion cells** — living, nucleated; closely associated with sieve tubes; control loading/unloading of food.
• **Phloem parenchyma** — storage.
• **Phloem fibres** — support (commercial fibres like jute/flax).

**Translocation**
• movement of food in phloem; can be **upward or downward** depending on need (bidirectional).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Phloem — Structure & Food Transport"?
• Explain: Phloem?
• State: Sieve tubes are living but lack nucleus at maturity; companion cells help m?
• State: Direction is from source (leaf) to sink (root/fruit/apical bud).?
• True or false trap: 'Phloem always moves food downward' is FALSE — bidirectional.?
• Exam focus: Match column: sieve tube ↔ food conduction; companion cell ↔ metabolic support of sieve tu?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Sieve plate — perforated end wall between sieve tube elements.
• Source — region producing/exporting food; Sink — region storing/using food.
• Phloem cells are mostly living; xylem vessels/tracheids are dead.
• 'Phloem always moves food downward' is FALSE — bidirectional.
• Girdling (removing phloem ring) blocks food to roots, not water (xylem is deeper).

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS — sieve tubes and companion cells.
• XYLEM: • Transports water and minerals • Direction: upward only (roots → leaves) • Made of dead, hollow cells • Driven by transpiration pull PHLOEM: • Transport
• Xylem consists of tracheids, vessels, wood (xylem) parenchyma and wood fibres — so Q and R are correct.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-04 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch1-06 — next topic
teacherTip: Phloem Feeds — Food, Living sieve tubes, bidirectional Flow.
examTip: Match column: sieve tube ↔ food conduction; companion cell ↔ metabolic support of sieve tube.

subtopic: 6. Ascent of Sap — How Water Reaches the Crown
content:
**Executive summary:** Ascent of sap: upward movement of water + dissolved minerals through xylem from roots to leaves (can reach 100+ m in trees).

**Must know**
• Transpiration pull = dominant mechanism in tall trees.
• Cohesion (water–water) + adhesion (water–xylem wall) maintain continuous water column.
• Root pressure causes guttation droplets on leaf margins (e.g. grass, tomato) in humid pre-dawn conditions.
**Ascent of sap**
• upward movement of water + dissolved minerals through xylem from roots to leaves (can reach 100+ m in trees).

**Forces involved:**
• **Root pressure** — active accumulation of ions in root xylem → water enters osmotically → positive pressure pushes sap upward; demonstrable by **bleeding/guttation** from cut stems at night; significant in small herbs, minor in tall trees.
• **Capillarity** — rise of water in narrow tubes due to adhesion + cohesion; contributes in narrow xylem but alone insufficient for tall trees.
• **Transpiration pull (cohesion–tension theory)** — MAIN force in tall plants: evaporation from leaves creates **tension**; water columns stay intact due to **cohesion** between water molecules; pull transmitted down the xylem like a chain.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Evaporation vs Transpiration vs Guttation**
| Process | Where | Form lost | Driver |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Evaporation | Any liquid surface | Water vapour | Temperature, humidity, wind |
| Transpiration | Leaf stomata (mainly) | Water vapour | Transpiration pull in xylem |
| Guttation | Hydathodes at leaf margins | Liquid droplets | Root pressure (humid nights) |


**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Ascent of Sap — How Water Reaches the Crown"?
• Explain: Transpiration pull?
• State: Cohesion (water–water) + adhesion (water–xylem wall) maintain continuous wa?
• State: Root pressure causes guttation droplets on leaf margins (e.g. grass, tomato?
• True or false trap: Guttation ≠ dew (dew is atmospheric condensation on surface).?
• True or false trap: Guttation ≠ transpiration (transpiration = water vapour through stomata).?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Ascent of sap — upward movement of xylem sap.
• Guttation — loss of liquid water as droplets from hydathodes when root pressure > transpiration.
• Guttation ≠ dew (dew is atmospheric condensation on surface).
• Guttation ≠ transpiration (transpiration = water vapour through stomata).
• 'Root pressure alone explains ascent in a 50 m tree' is FALSE.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• ASCENT OF SAP: the upward movement of water and minerals from the roots, through the xylem, to the aerial parts of the plant.
• TRANSPIRATION: The loss of water vapour from aerial parts of a plant, mainly through stomata on leaves.
• OSMOSIS: Movement of water through a semipermeable membrane from higher to lower water concentration.
• Ascent of sap takes place through xylem.
• Soil water enters root hair cells by OSMOSIS (semipermeable membrane).

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-05 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch1-07 — next topic
teacherTip: Tall trees: Transpiration Pull; Tiny herbs at night: Root Pressure — match mechanism to plant size and time.
examTip: 'Give reason water rises in xylem' — write transpiration pull + cohesion–tension in two lines; capillarity alone is incomplete.

content:
**Executive summary:** Transpiration: loss of water as water vapour from aerial parts of a plant, mainly through stomata on leaves (also lenticels, cuticle).

**Must know**
• >90% water absorbed is lost by transpiration — necessary 'cost' of photosynthesis.
• Stomata open in light (guard cells turgid); close in dark/drought to conserve water.
• Transpiration is essential for mineral distribution and cooling, not wasteful.
**Transpiration**
• loss of water as **water vapour** from aerial parts of a plant, mainly through **stomata** on leaves (also lenticels, cuticle).

**Process:**
• Mesophyll cells → water evaporates into intercellular spaces → diffuses out through stomatal pores when open.
• Creates **suction/tension** that pulls xylem sap upward (transpiration stream).

**Functions:**
• Absorbs minerals dissolved in soil (by pulling water through plant).
• Cools leaf surface (latent heat of vaporisation).
• Maintains cell turgidity and shape.
• Transports minerals to leaves.

**Factors increasing transpiration:** bright light (stomata open), high temperature, low humidity, wind/airflow, large leaf area.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Transpiration — Loss of Water Vapour"?
• Explain: >90% water absorbed is lost by transpiration?
• State: Stomata open in light (guard cells turgid); close in dark/drought to conser?
• State: Transpiration is essential for mineral distribution and cooling, not wastef?
• Exam focus: Polythene bag experiment proves transpiration — bag on leaf collects water vapour that con?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Stomata — pores on leaf epidermis flanked by guard cells regulating opening.
• Transpiration stream — continuous flow of water from soil → root → xylem → leaf → atmosphere.
• Transpiration (vapour, stomata) vs guttation (liquid, hydathodes).
• Wilting = excessive transpiration + inadequate water uptake.
• Xerophytes reduce transpiration: thick cuticle, sunken stomata, rolled leaves.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• TRANSPIRATION: The loss of water vapour from aerial parts of a plant, mainly through stomata on leaves.
• ASCENT OF SAP: the upward movement of water and minerals from the roots, through the xylem, to the aerial parts of the plant.
• OSMOSIS: Movement of water through a semipermeable membrane from higher to lower water concentration.
• XYLEM: • Transports water and minerals • Direction: upward only (roots → leaves) • Made of dead, hollow cells • Driven by transpiration pull PHLOEM: • Transport

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-06 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch1-08 — next topic
teacherTip: LIGHT opens stomata: Low humidity, Increased temperature, Gentle wind, High light — all boost transpiration.
examTip: Polythene bag experiment proves transpiration — bag on leaf collects water vapour that condenses; control leaf without bag shows none.

content:
**Executive summary:** Translocation: transport of dissolved organic food (photosynthate) through phloem from regions of production to regions of utilisation or storage.

**Must know**
• Translocation = phloem only; not xylem.
• Sucrose is main transported sugar (soluble, stable, non-reducing).
• Bidirectional — unlike xylem which is essentially unidirectional upward.
**Translocation**
• transport of **dissolved organic food** (photosynthate) through **phloem** from regions of **production** to regions of **utilisation or storage**.

**Source → Sink movement:**
• **Source** — mature photosynthesising leaves (main); also storage organs in spring (e.g. potato tuber sending sprouts).
• **Sink** — roots, developing fruits, seeds, apical meristems, young leaves.
• Direction can **reverse** seasonally (e.g. starch in roots mobilised upward in spring).

**Mechanism (overview):**
• Food loaded into phloem at source (active process, needs ATP).
• Mass flow along sieve tubes toward sink where food is unloaded and used/stored.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Translocation of Organic Food"?
• Explain: Translocation?
• State: Sucrose is main transported sugar (soluble, stable, non-reducing).?
• Explain: Bidirectional?
• True or false trap: Transport of water = xylem; transport of starch granules does NOT occur — starch is c?
• Exam focus: If girdled stem swells above the ring, food accumulates above blockage — classic proof of ?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Translocation — movement of organic solutes in phloem.
• Photosynthate — product of photosynthesis (sugars) transported to other tissues.
• Transport of water = xylem; transport of starch granules does NOT occur — starch is converted to sucrose before loading.
• Ringing/girdling experiment proves phloem carries food downward to roots.
• Young growing stem tip is a sink even though it has chlorophyll later.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• XYLEM: • Transports water and minerals • Direction: upward only (roots → leaves) • Made of dead, hollow cells • Driven by transpiration pull PHLOEM: • Transport

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-07 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch1-09 — next topic
teacherTip: Source has Surplus sugar; Sink Stores or uses — trace arrow from leaf to fruit/root in every diagram question.
examTip: If girdled stem swells **above** the ring, food accumulates above blockage — classic proof of downward phloem transport.

content:
**Executive summary:** Plants need 16 essential mineral elements obtained from soil as dissolved ions (absorbed by roots, often by active transport).

**Must know**
• Deficiency of one mineral cannot be corrected by supplying another — each has unique role.
• Nitrogen most commonly limiting in agriculture → why fertilisers are used.
• Minerals travel dissolved in xylem water to leaves.
**Macronutrients** (needed in larger amounts): C, H, O (from air/water), plus **N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S** from soil.
• **Nitrogen (N)** — proteins, chlorophyll, nucleic acids; deficiency → yellowing (chlorosis), stunted growth.
• **Phosphorus (P)** — ATP, DNA, cell membranes; deficiency → poor root growth, purple tinge.
• **Potassium (K)** — enzyme activation, stomatal opening; deficiency → brown leaf margins, weak stems.
• **Magnesium (Mg)** — centre of chlorophyll molecule; deficiency → interveinal chlorosis.
• **Calcium (Ca)** — cell wall (middle lamella); deficiency → tip burn in young leaves.

**Micronutrients** (trace): Fe, Mn, Cu, Zn, B, Mo, Cl, Ni — needed in tiny amounts but equally essential.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Essential Mineral Nutrients"?
• Explain: Deficiency of one mineral cannot be corrected by supplying another?
• State: Nitrogen most commonly limiting in agriculture → why fertilisers are used.?
• State: Minerals travel dissolved in xylem water to leaves.?
• True or false trap: 'Plants get carbon from soil' is FALSE — C from CO₂ in air.?
• Exam focus: Match deficiency symptom to mineral: yellow leaves → N or Mg; brown leaf edge → K; stunted?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Macronutrient — mineral required in relatively large quantity.
• Chlorosis — yellowing due to lack of chlorophyll (often N or Mg deficiency).
• Mg deficiency affects old leaves first (mobile); Ca deficiency affects young leaves (immobile).
• 'Plants get carbon from soil' is FALSE — C from CO₂ in air.
• Legumes fix atmospheric N₂ via Rhizobium in root nodules — reduces need for nitrogen fertiliser.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• MACRONUTRIENTS: Required in LARGE quantities.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-08 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch1-10 — next topic
teacherTip: NPK fertiliser bag = Nitrogen (leaves green), Phosphorus (roots/flowers), Potassium (overall vigour, disease resistance).
examTip: Match deficiency symptom to mineral: yellow leaves → N or Mg; brown leaf edge → K; stunted roots → P.

subtopic: 10. Key Experiments — Eosin, Girdling & Polythene Bag
content:
**Executive summary:** Eosin → xylem; Girdling → phloem; Polythene bag → transpiration. In girdling, swelling is ABOVE cut (food cannot pass down).

**Must know**
• Eosin → xylem; Girdling → phloem; Polythene bag → transpiration.
• In girdling, swelling is ABOVE cut (food cannot pass down).
• Dye does NOT travel in phloem in standard school eosin experiment.
**Eosin / safranin experiment (xylem pathway):**
• Cut stem of herb (e.g. balsam, celery) placed in dilute **eosin/safranin** solution (red dye).
• After few hours, dye appears in **xylem vessels** of stem and leaf veins — red streaks visible.
• Proves: water + dissolved substances move upward through **xylem**.

**Girdling / ringing experiment (phloem pathway):**
• Remove a **ring of bark** (including phloem) from woody stem; leave xylem (wood) intact.
• Swelling appears **above** the ring (food accumulates); roots below starve → plant may die.
• Proves: **phloem** (in bark) transports food **downward**; xylem (inner wood) still carries water upward.

**Polythene bag experiment (transpiration):**
• Transparent bag tied around a leafy branch; sealed at base around petiole.
• After sunlight: **water droplets** condense inside bag on inner surface.
• Control: bag on leafless twig — no condensation.
• Proves: leaves lose water vapour by **transpiration**.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Key Experiments — Eosin, Girdling & Polythene Bag"?
• State: Eosin → xylem; Girdling → phloem; Polythene bag → transpiration.?
• State: In girdling, swelling is ABOVE cut (food cannot pass down).?
• State: Dye does NOT travel in phloem in standard school eosin experiment.?
• True or false trap: Girdled plant wilts eventually because roots receive no food for respiration, not bec?
• True or false trap: Eosin stains dead xylem vessels red — living phloem sieve tubes are not the pathway h?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Girdling — surgical removal of a ring of phloem tissue (bark) without cutting xylem.
• Girdled plant wilts eventually because roots receive no food for respiration, not because water transport stops.
• Eosin stains dead xylem vessels red — living phloem sieve tubes are not the pathway here.
• Bag must be transparent and tied airtight at petiole — examiners ask 'why transparent?' → light for transpiration.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• (a) The process demonstrated is TRANSPIRATION.
• XYLEM: • Transports water and minerals • Direction: upward only (roots → leaves) • Made of dead, hollow cells • Driven by transpiration pull PHLOEM: • Transport
• In test tube A the water level falls because water is absorbed by the plant through its roots dipped in the water.
• ASCENT OF SAP: the upward movement of water and minerals from the roots, through the xylem, to the aerial parts of the plant.
• TRANSPIRATION: The loss of water vapour from aerial parts of a plant, mainly through stomata on leaves.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch1-09 — previous topic
teacherTip: E-X-P: Eosin = Xylem, girdling = Phloem — two classic proofs, never swap them in answers.
examTip: Draw labelled diagrams for all three experiments — ICSE often gives 3-mark 'describe and explain observation' questions.
